zones.name: Adaptive Web Services Shell
zones.description: Hosts the adaptive HTTP server, CLI surface, and domain gateways that expose backend capabilities to other packages.
insights: High cohesion (0.99) — files are tightly interconnected
Stabilize `packages/web/src/server/domain-gateway.ts` as the single ingress/egress point so future transports do not bypass shared policies.
Trim `packages/web/src/public.ts` exports to the contract actually used by dependents to keep the public surface disciplined.
Add integration tests around `packages/web/src/server/routes-adaptive.ts` to guard adaptive routing logic as new features land.
Extremely high cohesion (0.99) indicates the CLI, HTTP server, and domain gateways belong together—keep new backend utilities inside this shell to preserve that property.
Gateway files (`domain-gateway.ts`, `mcp-deps.ts`) already abstract external connectors; adding contract tests before onboarding integrations will keep coupling at its current 0.01 level.
`routes-adaptive.ts` centralizes adaptive route wiring; pair it with smoke tests whenever new adaptive surfaces are introduced to avoid regressions.
[call graph] 972 internal calls, 21 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 0.98, coupling: 0.02)

zones.name: Claude Foundation Client
zones.description: Shared client SDK that abstracts Anthropic CLI/API usage, retries, and telemetry for the rest of the stack.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Implements dual providers (API + CLI) so any caller gets transparent failover.
Centralizes token-usage parsing and project-config access for downstream analytics.

Provider selection logic in `packages/claude-client/src/create-client.ts:1` already encapsulates auto-detect/fallback, so standardize new consumers on this factory rather than instantiating providers directly to preserve consistent behavior.

Anthropic SDK integration in `packages/claude-client/src/api-provider.ts:21` exposes retry/backoff knobs; plumb those options through repo-level config before running heavier CI workloads so operators can tune quotas safely.

Token-usage normalization in `packages/claude-client/src/token-usage.ts:1` is the single source for billing data, so extend it whenever the SDK ships new usage fields (e.g., cache hits) to keep downstream cost analytics accurate.
[call graph] 317 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: CLI Orchestration Surface
zones.description: Top-level scripts that broker every n-dx workflow by spawning package CLIs and presenting a cohesive developer UX.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Holds the entrypoints (CLI, CI, web) that enforce the repo's four-tier dependency layering.
Shares config/help/test helpers so onboarding and diagnostics feel the same across commands.
Documented four-tier dependency rules in `cli.js:3` keep orchestration decoupled from execution; add an automated import-lint or test so future entrypoints cannot bypass the spawn-only contract.

Secret-aware config writing in `config.js:49` already chmods `.n-dx.json` when API keys exist, so route any new sensitive settings through this helper instead of ad-hoc package config writers to keep credentials locked down.

The command registry/search UX in `help.js:83` and its regression coverage in `tests/unit/help.test.js:4` should be treated as required touchpoints whenever commands are added or renamed to keep typo suggestions and docs accurate.
[call graph] 284 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Hench Agent Core
zones.description: Execution engine that assembles briefs, runs the agent loop, validates output, and summarizes autonomous work.
insights: High cohesion (0.95) — files are tightly interconnected
Re-exports lifecycle/planning/analysis APIs so upstream callers have a single surface.
Owns human-in-the-loop checkpoints like review gates and stuck-task detection.
Use `packages/hench/src/agent/index.ts:1` as the lone import surface for lifecycle, planning, and analysis primitives so CLI layers don’t deep-import internals and accidentally create cycles.

The review gate in `packages/hench/src/agent/analysis/review.ts:1` hardcodes a 30s prompt timeout; expose that through config or CLI flags before enabling unattended review flows, otherwise approvals can block runs.

Stuck-task heuristics in `packages/hench/src/agent/analysis/stuck.ts:1` depend on callers to pass a threshold, so document or set a default in the run orchestration to keep failure detection consistent across commands.
[call graph] 1824 internal calls, 2 outgoing, 27 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Hench Guard & Tools
zones.description: Security guardrails plus the tool-dispatch layer that mediates every filesystem, shell, git, and Rex action the agent performs.
insights: GuardRails centralizes command/path/git validation along with audit logging.
Tool dispatcher is the single interface Anthropics tool-calls can reach, keeping GuardError handling uniform.

GuardRails in `packages/hench/src/guard/index.ts:1` already enforces path, command, and git allowlists, so thread any new tool operations through this facade to keep policy enforcement and auditing centralized.

Session-aware limits in `packages/hench/src/guard/policy.ts:1` log every allow/deny decision; review or persist that audit log before raising rate/byte caps so you don’t lose visibility into abusive sequences.
Anthropic tool definitions live exclusively in `packages/hench/src/tools/dispatch.ts:1`, so register any new automation there to inherit GuardError handling instead of bypassing the dispatcher.
[call graph] 198 internal calls, 27 outgoing, 2 incoming (cohesion: 0.88, coupling: 0.12)

zones.name: Rex PRD Management CLI
zones.description: A self-contained CLI tool and MCP server for managing hierarchical product requirements documents, including code analysis, tree operations, validation, persistence, and AI-agent workflow integration.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 71% of project files (36/51) — may be too broad, consider splitting
The dependency graph flows cleanly from schema -> core -> store -> cli with no circular dependencies, indicating strong architectural discipline for a 36-file single-zone codebase.
The analyze module (scanners, propose, reconcile) is a well-isolated subsystem that could be extracted into its own zone if the project grows, since it has minimal coupling to core.
The MCP server in cli/mcp.ts at ~405 lines is the largest single file and serves as a second entry point alongside the CLI dispatcher, making it a natural seam for future zone splitting.

The store layer (file-adapter.ts) imports core/tree.js and core/canonical.js, creating a dependency inversion: store depends on core business logic instead of being a dumb persistence layer. Layering should be schema ← core ← store, but store reaches sideways into core.
PRIORITY_ORDER is duplicated three times (core/canonical.ts, core/next-task.ts, analyze/propose.ts) — a hidden coupling via copy-paste that risks drift if priority tiers change.
CLI commands init.ts and validate.ts bypass the store abstraction by directly using node:fs/promises for config file I/O, undermining the adapter pattern.
The core layer has no barrel export (no src/core/index.ts), so consumers import from individual files (core/tree.js, core/next-task.js), making the public API surface implicit rather than explicit.
REX_DIR constant is duplicated: declared in cli/commands/constants.ts and re-declared in cli/mcp.ts, creating a sync risk for the two entry points.

sortByPriority logic is duplicated between core/canonical.ts:14-27 (sortItems, recursive) and core/next-task.ts:11-18 (sortByPriority, shallow) — two subtly different sort implementations within the same core layer that can diverge.

MCP tool handlers in cli/mcp.ts reimplement the same orchestration logic as CLI commands (e.g., get_next_task duplicates cmdNext, update_task_status duplicates cmdUpdate) rather than sharing a common service layer, creating a parallel maintenance burden across ~400 lines.

The PRDItem interface in schema/v1.ts:21 has an index signature `[key: string]: unknown` alongside explicit fields, which defeats TypeScript's excess-property checking and allows arbitrary data to pass type checks silently — the `.passthrough()` on the Zod schema compounds this by accepting unknown keys at runtime too.

readLog() at store/file-adapter.ts:105 casts each parsed JSON line as LogEntry without validation (`JSON.parse(line) as LogEntry`), while appendLog() validates via Zod before writing — asymmetric serialization boundary means corrupted log files can inject malformed entries into the system.

The analyze --accept flow in cli/commands/analyze.ts:125-169 calls store.addItem() sequentially in a loop (one file write per item) with no batching or transaction, so a failure mid-loop leaves the PRD in a partially-updated state with no rollback.

update.ts:6-12 hardcodes VALID_STATUSES and VALID_PRIORITIES as string Sets instead of deriving them from the Zod enums in schema/validate.ts (ItemStatusSchema, PrioritySchema), creating a second source of truth that will drift if enum values change.
callSourceVisionMCP in recommend.ts:33-78 uses raw child_process.spawn with callback-based stream handling, but never sets a timeout — a hung sourcevision process will block the CLI indefinitely.

The PRDItemSchema in validate.ts:18 is typed as `z.ZodType<Record<string, unknown>>` erasing the inferred Zod type, so validateDocument returns `Record<string, unknown>` forcing every callsite to cast `as PRDDocument` — the Zod schema is well-typed internally but the export discards the type information.

PRIORITY_ORDER is defined three times (canonical.ts:7, next-task.ts:4, propose.ts:25) with two different type signatures — propose.ts uses Record<string, number> (loose) while the other two use Record<Priority, number> (strict), creating a silent type-safety gap
The adapter field in RexConfig is dead configuration — all 8 createStore() call sites hardcode the literal string "file" instead of reading config.adapter, making alternative adapters unreachable

Log event names for the same semantic operation differ between CLI and MCP: update.ts:68 emits "item_updated" while mcp.ts:129 emits "status_changed" for status changes, with no shared event-name constants
init.ts and validate.ts both bypass the store abstraction layer, using raw fs readFile/writeFile directly, making them incompatible with any non-file adapter
[call graph] 711 internal calls, 187 outgoing, 498 incoming (cohesion: 0.79, coupling: 0.21)

zones.name: Schema-Driven Viewer Surface
zones.description: Combines runtime schemas and viewer components to render and validate task data for the web UI.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Keep schema edits centralized in `packages/web/src/schema/v1.ts` and `validate.ts` so every viewer component consumes identical data contracts.
Apply shared component utilities (constants, copy-link button, collapsible sections) to enforce consistent UX patterns and reduce ad-hoc styles.
Introduce automated visual or interaction tests for panels like `active-tasks-panel.ts` and `breadcrumb.ts` to maintain trust in the high-cohesion UI layer.
Perfect cohesion and zero measured coupling show the schema + viewer stack is self-contained—treat it as the canonical place for UI-facing data contracts.
Schemas (`v1.ts`, `validate.ts`) already live with the components; documenting how components consume these helpers will keep future imports from leaking to backend internals.
Viewer panel components are numerous and critical; adopting visual regression checks will preserve quality as the component catalog grows.
[call graph] 1134 internal calls, 1 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Vendor-Agnostic LLM Adapter
zones.description: A higher-level client that selects Claude or Codex providers and loads unified LLM config for consumers such as hench.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Abstracts vendor choice so orchestration layers call one interface regardless of backend.
Provides Codex parity by adapting its CLI into the shared ClaudeClient contract.

Vendor-neutral parsing in `packages/llm-client/src/llm-config.ts:1` already merges legacy `claude` settings into the new `llm.*` schema, so route upcoming config migrations through this loader to avoid duplicating parsing logic elsewhere.

`packages/llm-client/src/llm-client.ts:1` routes requests by vendor and reuses Claude auth detection, which means any future vendor should be plugged in here so upstream callers stay ignorant of per-vendor quirks.

The Codex CLI provider in `packages/llm-client/src/codex-cli-provider.ts:1` shells out with temp files and retry/backoff; capture telemetry or expose config before tightening timeouts so agents can distinguish Codex issues from guard denials.
[call graph] 344 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)
Bidirectional coupling: "hench-agent-core" ↔ "hench-guard-and-tools" (8+10 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 137 files vs smallest with 6 — uneven decomposition
Layering from orchestration → execution → domain → foundation keeps the import graph acyclic and makes it easy to audit boundaries.
Guard/tool modules form the choke point for hench’s side effects, so safety improvements can focus on that seam to protect the rest of the system.
Viewer components in `schema-driven-viewer-surface` only touch the backend shell twice, so doubling down on schema-based DTOs can keep that boundary slim even as UI features expand.

Cross-zone imports show hench-2 → hench (10) and hench → hench-2 (8), so consider introducing thinner interfaces if you ever want the guard/tool layer to be deployable independently of the agent core.
Web zones only depend on each other twice (web-2 → web), keeping the UI stack modular; maintain that low coupling when adding dashboards so orchestration code stays back-end focused.

Current cross-zone imports (`schema-driven-viewer-surface` → `adaptive-web-services-shell`) are minimal; enforce that all future UI/server touchpoints flow through the published schema entrypoints to avoid creeping coupling.
[call graph] 27 calls: "hench-guard-and-tools" → "hench-agent-core"
[call graph] 21 calls: "adaptive-web-services-shell" → "packages-rex:rex-cli"
[call graph] 12 calls: "packages-sourcevision:tests" → "packages-sourcevision:cli"
